papers.title: Fairness in Large Language Models: A Taxonomic Survey
papers.authors: Zhibo Chu, Zichong Wang, Wenbin Zhang
papers.overlap: Direct parent lineage — taxonomizes fairness in LLMs that answer (intrinsic/extrinsic bias, debiasing generation). Senior author Wenbin Zhang is a co-author on our draft. Provides our definitional grounding and venue anchor.
papers.how_ours_differs: Ours is the explicit agentic successor: Chu et al. cover single-turn QA and static generation only; they do not treat tool selection, memory/RAG, multi-agent delegation, planning, user modeling, or long-horizon drift. Our survey takes the agentic pipeline as its organizing axis, which Chu et al. explicitly do not address.

papers.title: Evaluation and Benchmarking of LLM Agents: A Survey
papers.authors: Mahmoud Mohammadi, Yipeng Li, Jane Lo, Wendy Yip
papers.overlap: Surveys evaluation methodology for LLM agents broadly; fairness appears as a brief sub-subsection (3.4.1) inside the Safety and Alignment chapter, estimated at a few paragraphs of a 40+ page document. Uses a two-dimensional taxonomy (evaluation objectives × evaluation process). Explicitly cited in our draft as a near-neighbor (mohammadi2025evaluation).
papers.how_ours_differs: Fairness is one tiny sub-topic among many evaluation objectives; our survey takes fairness as the *sole* organizing axis. The paper does not organize by agent component, does not propose action-level disparity metrics, and does not synthesize bias mechanisms per component.

papers.title: Agentic AI in Healthcare & Medicine: A Seven-Dimensional Taxonomy for Empirical Evaluation of LLM-based Agents
papers.authors: Shubham Vatsal, Harsh Dubey, Aditi Singh
papers.overlap: Proposes a seven-dimensional taxonomy for evaluating LLM agents in healthcare; fairness is one of seven dimensions (not the organizing axis) and the treatment is largely conceptual rather than empirically grounded. Explicitly cited in our draft as a near-neighbor (vatsal2026agentic).
papers.how_ours_differs: Single domain (healthcare), fairness is one of seven dimensions, no component-organized bias analysis, no action-level metrics, no cross-domain or multi-component synthesis. Ours is fairness-dedicated, cross-domain, and tied to executable CFR/MASD/action-disparity instruments.

papers.title: Counterfactual Fairness Evaluation of LLM-Based Contact Center Agent Quality Assurance System
papers.authors: Kawin Mayilvaghanan, Siddhant Gupta, Ayush Kumar
papers.overlap: Applies CFR and MASD metrics — the exact metrics we generalize — to a single-domain (contact center QA) LLM agent. Introduces the 13-dimension taxonomy (Identity, Context, Behavioral Style) for that one use case. Explicitly cited in our draft as mayilvaghanan2026counterfactual.
papers.how_ours_differs: Restricted to one domain and one agent role (QA evaluator in contact centers). No component decomposition across tool selection, memory, multi-agent, planning, user modeling, or long-horizon. Our survey generalizes CFR/MASD across all six agent components and multiple consequential domains — this is a specific single-domain application paper, not a survey.

papers.title: A Survey on Trustworthy LLM Agents: Threats and Countermeasures
papers.authors: Miao Yu, Fanci Meng, Xinyun Zhou, Shilong Wang, Junyuan Mao, Linsey Pang, Tianlong Chen, Kun Wang, Xinfeng Li, Yongfeng Zhang, Bo An, Qingsong Wen
papers.overlap: Surveys trustworthiness of LLM agents including safety, privacy, truthfulness, and fairness across intrinsic components (brain, memory, tool) and extrinsic aspects (user, agent, environment). Overlaps structurally with our component anatomy.
papers.how_ours_differs: Fairness is one of five or more trustworthiness dimensions alongside safety, privacy, robustness, etc. The organizing axis is threats-and-countermeasures, not fairness mechanisms by component. Our survey is fairness-only, provides a dedicated evaluation framework (CFR/MASD/action-level disparity), a mitigation map tied to components, and an empirical bias audit — none of which appear in this paper.

papers.title: Fairness in Agentic AI: A Unified Framework for Ethical and Equitable Multi-Agent System
papers.authors: Rajesh Ranjan, Shailja Gupta, Surya Narayan Singh
papers.overlap: Self-described as 'a comprehensive survey of fairness in multi-agent AI' with a unified framework treating fairness as an emergent property of agent interactions. Addresses emergent biases, systemic inefficiencies, conflicting incentives, fairness constraints, and bias mitigation strategies in multi-agent systems.
papers.how_ours_differs: This is only 12 pages (4 figures, 1 table) — a short position/framework paper, not a substantive survey. Crucially, it treats classical decentralized multi-agent systems broadly rather than LLM-based agents specifically. It does not organize fairness by the LLM agent pipeline components (tool selection, memory/RAG, multi-agent delegation, planning, user modeling, long-horizon drift). It has no action-level disparity metrics, no evaluation framework, no empirical audit, no mitigation map by component, and no consequential-domain grounding. Our survey is 60+ pages with 127 cited papers, original cross-framework audit, and a complete evaluation + mitigation framework.

papers.title: A Survey on Reliability, Transparency, Accountability, and Fairness in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems through the Responsibility Lens
papers.overlap: 39-page survey examining LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems through four pillars: Reliability, Transparency, Accountability, and Fairness and Ethics. Covers coordination patterns (debate, critique, aggregation, routing), governance, logging, and fairness mechanisms across 60+ works from 2023-2025.
papers.how_ours_differs: Fairness is one of four RTAF pillars, not the organizing axis. The paper covers LLM-MAS broadly through a responsibility lens; it does not decompose fairness by the six agent components (tool selection, memory, delegation, planning, user modeling, long-horizon), does not propose action-level disparity metrics, and does not include an empirical audit. No arXiv ID found — not indexable as a competing arXiv preprint. Our survey provides the dedicated component-organized fairness taxonomy that this paper's fairness pillar sketches only briefly.

papers.title: Interactional Fairness in LLM Multi-Agent Systems: An Evaluation Framework
papers.overlap: Introduces interactional fairness evaluation (interpersonal + informational fairness) for LLM multi-agent negotiation tasks, drawing on organizational psychology. Addresses how agent communication tone and justification quality affect fairness outcomes.
papers.how_ours_differs: Narrow scope: covers only inter-agent communication fairness in resource negotiation, not demographic/protected-group fairness in LLM agents. Does not address tool selection, memory/RAG, planning, user modeling, or long-horizon bias. The fairness concept is organizational-justice fairness (procedural/interactional), not group/individual/counterfactual fairness. Not a survey. Orthogonal to our component-organized demographic bias framework.

papers.title: Emergent Bias and Fairness in Multi-Agent Decision Systems
papers.authors: Maeve Madigan, Parameswaran Kamalaruban, Glenn Moynihan, Tom Kempton, David Sutton, Stuart Burrell
papers.overlap: Empirically investigates fairness risks in multi-agent systems for financial decisions (credit scoring, income estimation). Demonstrates that bias patterns cannot be traced to individual agent components and that the collective can be more or less biased than members — relevant to our §3.7 agentic multiplier argument. This paper is already cited in our draft as madigan2025emergent.
papers.how_ours_differs: Not a survey. Narrow domain (financial tabular). Its key finding — bias patterns cannot be reduced to individual components — is an empirical result we cite as evidence for the need for trajectory-level evaluation, one of our open-agenda points. Our survey synthesizes this and related work into a broader component taxonomy, not a single-domain simulation study.

papers.title: Responsible Agentic Reasoning and AI Agents: A Critical Survey
papers.authors: Shaina Raza, Ranjan Sapkota, Manoj Karkee, Christos Emmanouilidis
papers.overlap: Surveys responsible agentic AI (R2A2 framework) covering reasoning methods (CoT, ReAct, tree-of-thought, tool use, memory, RAG, agentic browsing) and responsible AI principles (fairness, privacy, transparency, accountability). Covers studies from January 2023 to November 2025.
papers.how_ours_differs: Organizing axis is responsible reasoning (fairness + privacy + safety + transparency + accountability together), not fairness alone. Fairness is one of five responsible AI dimensions. Does not organize by agent component from a fairness perspective, does not propose action-level disparity metrics, does not include an empirical audit or domain-specific harm evidence (hiring, lending, healthcare). Not indexed on arXiv. Our survey is fairness-dedicated and component-organized.

papers.title: TRiSM for Agentic AI: A Review of Trust, Risk, and Security Management in LLM-based Agentic Multi-Agent Systems
papers.authors: Shaina Raza, Ranjan Sapkota, Manoj Karkee, Christos Emmanouilidis
papers.overlap: Reviews Trust, Risk, and Security Management in LLM-based multi-agent systems, with five pillars: Explainability, ModelOps, Security, Privacy, Lifecycle Governance. Proposes two novel metrics (Component Synergy Score, Tool Utilization Efficacy).
papers.how_ours_differs: Does not cover fairness/equity/bias as a dedicated topic — fairness is absent from the five TRiSM pillars. The organizing axis is security/risk management, not demographic fairness. No connection to group/individual/counterfactual fairness frameworks or action-level disparity. Distinct from our work.

papers.title: Towards trustworthy agentic AI: a comprehensive survey of safety, robustness, privacy, and system security
papers.authors: Jinhu Qi, Muzhi Li, Jiahong Liu, Yuqin Shu, Dianzhi Yu, Shicheng Ma, Wenqian Cui, Yiyang Zhao, Yiyi Chen, Ruoxi Jiang, Irwin King, Zenglin Xu
papers.overlap: Comprehensive survey of trustworthy agentic AI through two core pillars: Safety/Robustness and Privacy/System Security. Mentions fairness and accountability explicitly as 'relevant context rather than parallel chapters.'
papers.how_ours_differs: Fairness is explicitly secondary — the authors themselves state fairness is discussed as context, not a parallel chapter. The organizing axis is safety + privacy/security threats. No component-organized fairness analysis, no action-level disparity metrics, no empirical fairness audit. Distinct from our work.

papers.title: Towards a framework for Bias Evaluation of AI Agents in Agentic Workflows
papers.venue: Proceedings of the 2026 Conference on Human Centred Artificial Intelligence — Education and Practice (ACM DL DOI: 10.1145/3777490.3777504)
papers.overlap: Proposes an evaluation framework for bias in agentic workflows using demographic swapping, contamination prevention, and statistical analysis for multi-agent workflows. Reports bias amplification factors up to 1.51x in sequential agent interactions. Addresses EU AI Act Article 10 compliance.
papers.how_ours_differs: Framework paper focused on evaluation methodology for multi-stage agentic bias (amplification in workflows), not a survey. Does not cover the full spectrum of agent components, does not propose CFR/MASD or action-level metrics, and is primarily a compliance/audit tool rather than an organizing taxonomy of where bias enters the agent pipeline. Not a broad survey; no arXiv ID found.

papers.title: MALIBU Benchmark: Multi-Agent LLM Implicit Bias Uncovered
papers.authors: Imran Mirza, Cole Huang, Ishwara Vasista, Rohan Patil, Asli Akalin, Sean O'Brien, Kevin Zhu
papers.venue: Building Trust in LLMs @ ICLR 2025; NAACL SRW 2025 (arXiv:2507.01019)
papers.overlap: Benchmark paper uncovering implicit bias in multi-agent LLM assignment scenarios using demographic personas (gender, race, religion). Shows multi-agent systems can reproduce and amplify biases. Cited in our draft as mirza2025malibu.
papers.how_ours_differs: Not a survey. Covers one agent component (multi-agent role/task assignment) and only the persona-induced bias dimension. Does not cover tool selection, memory/RAG, planning, user modeling, or long-horizon bias. Our survey situates MALIBU as evidence within the multi-agent delegation component of a broader component taxonomy.

papers.title: Fairness in Multi-Agent Systems for Software Engineering: An SDLC-Oriented Rapid Review
papers.authors: Corey Yang-Smith, Ronnie de Souza Santos, Ahmad Abdellatif
papers.overlap: Rapid review (18 retained from 350) of fairness in LLM multi-agent systems for software development. Characterizes fairness through trustworthy AI principles, bias reduction, and interactional dynamics. Identifies gaps: fragmented evaluation, limited generalization, scarce governance.
papers.how_ours_differs: Domain-restricted to software engineering; rapid review methodology (only 18 papers); does not organize by agent component (tool selection, memory, planning, user modeling, long-horizon); does not propose action-level disparity metrics or an evaluation framework. Workshop paper. Our survey is cross-domain, 127 papers, with a full component taxonomy and evaluation/mitigation frameworks.

papers.title: Bias and Unfairness in Information Retrieval Systems: New Challenges in the LLM Era
papers.authors: Sunhao Dai, Chen Xu, Shicheng Xu, Liang Pang, Zhenhua Dong, Jun Xu
papers.venue: KDD 2024 Tutorial & Survey; also WSDM 2025 tutorial (arXiv:2404.11457)
papers.overlap: Surveys fairness/bias in IR systems augmented with LLMs (search, reranking, RAG as a retrieval pipeline). Covers our §3.2 Memory and Retrieval component partially, since RAG is a retrieval system.
papers.how_ours_differs: Covers static IR systems (search engines, recommenders), not autonomous LLM agents. Does not address tool selection, multi-agent delegation, planning, user modeling, or long-horizon agent behavior. Framing is retrieval-system bias, not agent-component bias. Our survey is the agent-side complement to this IR-side survey.

papers.title: Navigating the Risks: A Survey of Security, Privacy, and Ethics Threats in LLM-Based Agents
papers.authors: Yuyou Gan, Yong Yang, Zhe Ma, Ping He, Rui Zeng, Yiming Wang, Qingming Li, Chunyi Zhou, Songze Li, Ting Wang, Yunjun Gao, Yingcai Wu, Shouling Ji
papers.overlap: Surveys security, privacy, and ethics threats in LLM agents via a taxonomy based on sources and impacts. Ethics section includes bias. Notes that bias in LLM-based agents involves the LLM-based controller and multi-modal inputs/outputs.
papers.how_ours_differs: Organizing axis is security/privacy/ethics threats, not fairness. Bias receives brief treatment within a broad 'ethics' category alongside many other threat types. No component-organized fairness taxonomy, no action-level metrics, no empirical fairness audit. Our survey goes substantially deeper into each fairness-relevant component with dedicated mechanisms, evidence, and measurement instruments.

papers.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents
papers.authors: Asaf Yehudai, Lilach Eden, Alan Li, Guy Uziel, Yilun Zhao, Roy Bar-Haim, Arman Cohan, Michal Shmueli-Scheuer
papers.overlap: Comprehensive survey of LLM agent evaluation through five perspectives: core capabilities, application-specific benchmarks, generalist evaluation, benchmark dimensions, and evaluation frameworks. Mentions bias mitigation as a gap in current benchmarks.
papers.how_ours_differs: Not focused on fairness at all — fairness/bias appears as an explicitly identified gap ('evaluations still lack comprehensive tests for bias mitigation'). The organizational axis is evaluation methodology, not fairness mechanisms. Our survey fills precisely the gap this paper calls out.

threat_assessment: No found paper genuinely scoops the core novelty claim of our survey: a dedicated, agent-component-organized fairness taxonomy of LLM-based agents covering all six components (tool/API selection, memory and retrieval, multi-agent delegation, planning and decomposition, user modeling, and long-horizon drift), paired with a generalized evaluation framework (CFR/MASD/action-level disparity), a mitigation map, and an original empirical audit. The closest candidates and honest assessments follow. arXiv:2502.07254 (Ranjan et al., Feb 2025) is the most title-similar paper — it self-describes as 'a comprehensive survey of fairness in multi-agent AI.' However, it is only 12 pages and 1 table, treats classical decentralized multi-agent systems broadly (not LLM-based agents specifically), proposes no component-organized taxonomy, offers no action-level metrics or empirical audit, and does not cite the LLM agent literature (ReAct, Toolformer, AutoGen, RAG, etc.). It is a position/framework sketch, not a substantive survey — a CSUR reviewer would not consider it overlapping at publication depth. The Ebrahimi-Asudeh RTAF survey (2025, ResearchGate-only) covers fairness as one of four pillars in LLM-MAS; at 39 pages it is the most structurally similar work, but fairness is one pillar among four (reliability, transparency, accountability, fairness), not the organizing axis. No arXiv ID is discoverable, suggesting it has not been submitted to major venues. Our survey's fairness-only organization, six-component taxonomy, evaluation framework, and empirical audit are substantially more comprehensive on the fairness dimension than this paper's single pillar. All other found papers are either (a) broad trustworthiness surveys where fairness is one secondary dimension (arXiv:2503.09648 TrustAgent; arXiv:2605.23989 Qi et al.; arXiv:2506.04133 TRiSM; arXiv:2507.21504 Mohammadi et al.), (b) single-domain empirical papers not surveys (arXiv:2602.14970 Mayilvaghanan; arXiv:2512.16433 Madigan et al.; arXiv:2507.01019 MALIBU), (c) domain-restricted rapid reviews (arXiv:2604.13103 Yang-Smith et al. — software engineering only), (d) IR-focused not agent-focused (arXiv:2404.11457 Dai et al.), or (e) healthcare-specific with fairness as one of seven dimensions (arXiv:2602.04813 Vatsal et al.). The CSUR reviewers' most natural challenge will be arXiv:2502.07254 and the Ebrahimi-Asudeh RTAF survey; the response is already written into our §1.4 (Positioning) — both treat fairness as one dimension or use a classical-MAS rather than LLM-pipeline framing, and neither delivers component-organized analysis at survey depth. The originality claim of 'first dedicated survey of fairness in LLM-based agents organized by agent component' holds as of June 2026.
